title: Economics Vocabulary
description: Key economic terms, concepts, and principles

cards.back: The amount of a good or service that producers are willing to sell at various prices
The amount of a good or service that consumers are willing to buy at various prices
When demand increases and supply stays the same, prices rise; when supply increases and demand stays the same, prices fall
The price at which supply equals demand
Equilibrium price
The fundamental economic problem: unlimited wants but limited resources
Scarcity
The value of the next-best alternative you give up when making a choice
The total value of all goods and services produced in a country in a given period

cards.front: Total value of goods and services in a country
cards.back: GDP (Gross Domestic Product)
A general increase in prices and decrease in the purchasing power of money

cards.front: General rise in prices over time
cards.back: Inflation
A general decrease in prices (opposite of inflation)
A period of declining economic activity, typically defined as two consecutive quarters of falling GDP

cards.front: Two consecutive quarters of falling GDP
cards.back: Recession
A severe, prolonged recession with high unemployment and falling output
A market with only one seller who controls the supply of a good or service
Monopoly
A market dominated by a few large sellers

cards.front: Market dominated by a few large sellers
cards.back: Oligopoly
A market with many sellers offering identical products; no single seller can influence prices
Government decisions about spending and taxation to influence the economy
Fiscal policy
Central bank actions (like changing interest rates) to control the money supply

cards.front: Central bank controlling the money supply
cards.back: Monetary policy
The central bank of the United States; controls monetary policy

cards.front: Central bank of the United States
cards.back: The Federal Reserve (the Fed)
The cost of borrowing money, expressed as a percentage
A tax on imported goods
Tariff
Trade between countries without tariffs, quotas, or other restrictions
Economic system where private individuals own the means of production and operate for profit
Economic system where the government or community owns the means of production
System where all property is publicly owned and each person works and is paid according to their needs (based on Marx)
Father of modern economics; wrote The Wealth of Nations (1776); advocated free markets and the 'invisible hand'

cards.front: The Wealth of Nations — who wrote it?
cards.back: Adam Smith (1776)
Adam Smith's idea that individual self-interest in a free market benefits society as a whole
A person who starts and runs a business, taking on financial risk for profit
Land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship
The percentage of the labor force that is jobless and actively seeking work
Measures the average change in prices paid by consumers for goods and services; used to track inflation
Consumer Price Index (CPI)
When government spending exceeds revenue (taxes) in a given period
The total amount of money a government owes from accumulated deficits
When a country imports more than it exports
Trade deficit
A share of ownership in a company
A loan an investor makes to a government or corporation, which pays back with interest
Stock = ownership in a company; Bond = a loan you make to a company/government
